message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>On the present trajectory, COVID is inevitably becoming a global epidemic, leading to concerns regarding the pandemic potential in China and other countries.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In this study, we use the time-dependent reproduction number (R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    ) to comprise the COVID transmissibility across different countries.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We used data from Jan 20, 2019, to Feb 29, 2020, on the number of newly confirmed cases, obtained from the reports published by the CDC, to infer the incidence of infectious over time. A two-step procedure was used to estimate the R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    . The first step used data on known index-secondary cases pairs, from publicly available case reports, to estimate the serial interval distribution. The second step estimated the R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    jointly from the incidence data and the information data in the first step. R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    was then used to simulate the epidemics across all major cities in China and typical countries worldwide.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Based on a total of 126 index-secondary cases pairs from 4 international regions, we estimated that the serial interval for SARS-2-CoV was 4.18 (IQR 1.92 – 6.65) days. Domestically, R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    of China, Hubei province, Wuhan had fallen below 1.0 on 9 Feb, 10 Feb and 13 Feb (R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    were 0.99±0.02, 0.99±0.02 and 0.96±0.02), respectively. Internationally, as of 26 Feb, statistically significant periods of COVID spread (R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    >1) were identified for most regions, except for Singapore (R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    was 0.92±0.17).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The epidemic in China has been well controlled, but the worldwide pandemic has not been well controlled. Worldwide preparedness and vulnerability against COVID-19 should be regarded with more care.</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:title>What is already known on this subject?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The basic reproduction number (R0) and the-time-dependent reproduction number (Rt) are two important indicators of infectious disease transmission. In addition, Rt as a derivative of R0 could be used to assess the epidemiological development of the disease and effectiveness of control measures. Most current researches used data from earlier periods in Wuhan and refer to the epidemiological features of SARS, which are possibly biased. Meanwhile, there are fewer studies discussed the Rt of COVID-19. Current clinical and epidemiological data are insufficient to help us understand the full view of the potential transmission of this disease.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:title>What this study adds?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>We use up-to-data observation of the serial interval and cases arising from local transmission to calculate the Rt in different outbreak level area and every province in China as well as five-top sever outbreak countries and other overseas. By comparing the Rt, we discussed the situation of outbreak around the world.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: China’s effective control and other countries’ uncharted challenge against COVID-19: an epidemiological and modelling study
